Spatial Regression

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source

Spatial Epidemiology — Lehigh University

Author

Eric Delmelle

Published

January 1, 2026

Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

  1. Explain why OLS fails when spatial autocorrelation is present in the residuals
  2. Distinguish between a spatial lag model (SAR), a spatial error model (SEM), and their extensions
  3. Use Lagrange Multiplier tests to choose between model specifications
  4. Fit and interpret four core spatial regression models in R
  5. Compute and explain direct and indirect (spillover) effects in spatial models
  6. Appreciate the implications of spatial spillovers for public health policy

1. Why Spatial Regression?

In earlier sessions we tested for spatial autocorrelation using Moran’s I and Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA). We found that health outcomes — disease rates, accessibility scores, socioeconomic exposures — are rarely spatially random. Nearby places tend to be more alike than distant places.

This is not just a descriptive curiosity. When we model health outcomes using Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression, we assume that the residuals are independent across observations. That assumption almost never holds for spatial health data. Ignoring it has real consequences:

  • Standard errors are biased, leading to incorrect inference
  • Coefficients can be inconsistent (SAR case)
  • We may miss important spillover effects — mechanisms by which conditions in one place influence outcomes in neighboring places

Spatial regression models explicitly account for these dependencies. Rather than being a nuisance correction, spatial structure often carries substantive meaning.

1.1 A Running Example: Liquor Sales and Social Harm

We use the NCVACO dataset — county-level data from North Carolina and Virginia — originally assembled by economist Mark Burkey to study the geography of alcohol markets. This dataset is analytically rich because it contains:

  • A plausible outcome variable: DUI arrests per 1,000 adults (DUI1802)
  • Access measures: distance to nearest liquor outlet (BKGRTOABC), distance to nearest bar (BKGRTOMIX)
  • Socioeconomic covariates: per capita income, unemployment, college enrollment, poverty
  • Cultural variables: % Southern Baptist (BAPTISTSP), % armed forces (ARMEDP)

The spatial dimension is central: liquor market regulation varies sharply across state borders; commuting patterns connect counties; enforcement intensity may spill across county lines.

Note

Data source: Burkey, M.L. (2010). Geographic Access and Demand in the Market for Alcohol. The Review of Regional Studies, 40(2), 159–179. Data available via BurkeyAcademy’s GIS & Spatial Econometrics Project.

2. Data Setup

Code 
library(sf)
library(spdep)
library(spatialreg)
library(tidyverse)
library(tmap)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
Code 
# Load the NCVACO dataset bundled with spatialreg
data("boston", package = "spatialreg")  # placeholder check
data("nc.sids", package = "spData", envir = environment())

# The NCVACO data is distributed with the spdep package as a shapefile
# We load it directly from the package
nc_path <- system.file("shapes/columbus.shp", package = "spData")

# Use Columbus Ohio crime data (bundled, similar structure) as a 
# self-contained teaching substitute — same model family, clean reproducibility
columbus <- st_read(system.file("shapes/columbus.shp", package = "spData"),
                    quiet = TRUE)
Tip

Self-contained reproducibility: This document uses the columbus dataset bundled with the spData package — no external file downloads required. The Columbus dataset contains neighborhood-level crime, income, and housing data for Columbus, Ohio, and is a classic benchmark in spatial econometrics. The same model logic applies directly to the NCVACO alcohol dataset or any county-level health outcome data.

2.1 Variable Overview

The Columbus dataset contains 49 neighborhoods with the following key variables:

Variable Description
CRIME Residential burglaries and vehicle thefts per 1,000 households
INC Household income (in $1,000s)
HOVAL Housing value (in $1,000s)
OPEN Open space in neighborhood
PLUMB % housing units without plumbing
DISCBD Distance to Central Business District
Code 
columbus |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(CRIME, INC, HOVAL, OPEN, PLUMB, DISCBD) |>
  summary() |>
  kable(caption = "Summary statistics: Columbus neighborhood dataset") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Summary statistics: Columbus neighborhood dataset
CRIME INC HOVAL OPEN PLUMB DISCBD
Min. : 0.18 Min. : 4.48 Min. :17.9 Min. : 0.00 Min. : 0.133 Min. :0.37
1st Qu.:20.05 1st Qu.: 9.96 1st Qu.:25.7 1st Qu.: 0.26 1st Qu.: 0.332 1st Qu.:1.70
Median :34.00 Median :13.38 Median :33.5 Median : 1.01 Median : 1.024 Median :2.67
Mean :35.13 Mean :14.38 Mean :38.4 Mean : 2.77 Mean : 2.364 Mean :2.85
3rd Qu.:48.59 3rd Qu.:18.32 3rd Qu.:43.3 3rd Qu.: 3.94 3rd Qu.: 2.534 3rd Qu.:3.89
Max. :68.89 Max. :31.07 Max. :96.4 Max. :25.00 Max. :18.811 Max. :5.57

2.2 Spatial Distribution of Crime

Code 
tmap_mode("plot")

tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("CRIME",
          style = "quantile",
          n = 5,
          palette = "YlOrRd",
          title = "Crime per 1,000 HH") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(
    main.title = "Residential Crime, Columbus OH",
    main.title.size = 1,
    legend.outside = FALSE,
    frame = FALSE
  )

3. Constructing Spatial Weights

Before fitting any spatial model, we need to define who is a neighbor of whom, and how to weight those relationships.

3.1 Queen Contiguity

Two polygons are queen contiguous if they share any boundary point (including corners). This is the most common choice for areal data.

Code 
# Queen contiguity neighbor list
queen_nb <- poly2nb(columbus, queen = TRUE)

# Rook contiguity (shared edges only, no corners)
rook_nb <- poly2nb(columbus, queen = FALSE)

# Convert to row-standardized listw object
queen_listw <- nb2listw(queen_nb, style = "W")
rook_listw  <- nb2listw(rook_nb,  style = "W")

# Summary
summary(queen_nb)
Neighbour list object:
Number of regions: 49 
Number of nonzero links: 236 
Percentage nonzero weights: 9.829 
Average number of links: 4.816 
Link number distribution:

 2  3  4  5  6  7  8  9 10 
 5  9 12  5  9  3  4  1  1 
5 least connected regions:
1 6 42 46 47 with 2 links
1 most connected region:
20 with 10 links
Note

Row standardization (style = "W") means each row of the weights matrix sums to 1. This makes the spatial lag \(\mathbf{Wy}\) interpretable as a local average of neighbors’ values — convenient for interpretation but it does change the scale of \(\rho\).

3.2 Symmetry Check

Code 
is.symmetric.nb(queen_nb)
[1] TRUE
Code 
is.symmetric.nb(rook_nb)
[1] TRUE

Both contiguity structures are symmetric: if A is a neighbor of B, then B is a neighbor of A. This is a prerequisite for many spatial regression estimators.

3.3 Visualizing the Neighbor Network

Code 
# Get centroids for plotting
coords <- st_centroid(st_geometry(columbus))

plot(st_geometry(columbus), border = "grey70", col = "grey95",
     main = "Queen Contiguity Neighbor Network")
plot(queen_nb, coords, add = TRUE, col = "#2196F3", lwd = 0.8)

4. Testing for Spatial Autocorrelation

Before choosing a spatial model, we should confirm that spatial autocorrelation is actually present in our outcome and, after OLS, in our residuals.

4.1 Moran’s I on the Outcome

Code 
moran_crime <- moran.test(columbus$CRIME, listw = queen_listw)
moran_crime

    Moran I test under randomisation

data:  columbus$CRIME  
weights: queen_listw    

Moran I statistic standard deviate = 5.6, p-value = 0.00000001
alternative hypothesis: greater
sample estimates:
Moran I statistic       Expectation          Variance 
         0.500189         -0.020833          0.008689
Code 
cat(sprintf(
  "Moran's I = %.4f  |  p-value = %.4e\n",
  moran_crime$estimate["Moran I statistic"],
  moran_crime$p.value
))
Moran's I = 0.5002  |  p-value = 1.1394e-08

A positive, significant Moran’s I confirms spatial clustering in the crime rate — high-crime neighborhoods tend to be surrounded by other high-crime neighborhoods.

5. The Four Core Spatial Regression Models

All spatial regression models in the classical framework begin from a standard linear specification and then add spatial structure. We define our regression equation once:

\[\text{CRIME}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{INC}_i + \beta_2 \cdot \text{HOVAL}_i + \varepsilon_i\]

Code 
reg_eq <- CRIME ~ INC + HOVAL

5.1 Model 1: OLS (Baseline)

OLS is our benchmark. We estimate it first, then test whether its residuals are spatially autocorrelated.

Code 
reg1 <- lm(reg_eq, data = columbus)
summary(reg1)

Call:
lm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-34.42  -6.39  -1.58   9.05  28.65 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)   68.619      4.735   14.49  < 2e-16 ***
INC           -1.597      0.334   -4.78 0.000018 ***
HOVAL         -0.274      0.103   -2.65    0.011 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 11.4 on 46 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.552, Adjusted R-squared:  0.533 
F-statistic: 28.4 on 2 and 46 DF,  p-value: 0.00000000934

Residual spatial autocorrelation test:

Code 
lm_moran <- lm.morantest(reg1, listw = queen_listw)
lm_moran

    Global Moran I for regression residuals

data:  
model: lm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus)
weights: queen_listw

Moran I statistic standard deviate = 2.8, p-value = 0.002
alternative hypothesis: greater
sample estimates:
Observed Moran I      Expectation         Variance 
        0.222109        -0.033418         0.008099

Significant residual autocorrelation confirms that OLS is misspecified — something spatial is going on that our covariates do not capture.

Code 
columbus$resid_ols <- residuals(reg1)

tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("resid_ols",
          style = "jenks", n = 5,
          palette = "RdBu", midpoint = 0,
          title = "OLS residuals") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(
    main.title = "OLS Residuals",
    main.title.size = 1,
    frame = FALSE,
    legend.outside = FALSE
  )

5.2 Model Selection via LM Tests

The Lagrange Multiplier (LM) tests are the standard decision tool for choosing between a spatial lag and a spatial error specification.

Code 
lm_tests <- lm.LMtests(reg1, queen_listw,
                        test = c("LMerr", "LMlag", "RLMerr", "RLMlag", "SARMA"))
summary(lm_tests)
    Lagrange multiplier diagnostics for spatial dependence
data:  
model: lm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus)
weights: queen_listw
 
       statistic parameter p.value   
LMerr     5.2062         1  0.0225 * 
LMlag     8.8980         1  0.0029 **
RLMerr    0.0439         1  0.8340   
RLMlag    3.7357         1  0.0533 . 
SARMA     8.9419         2  0.0114 * 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Important

Decision rule (Anselin 1988):

  1. If only LMlag is significant → use SAR (spatial lag model)
  2. If only LMerr is significant → use SEM (spatial error model)
  3. If both are significant → compare the robust versions (RLMlag vs. RLMerr); choose the one with the smaller p-value
  4. If neither is significant → OLS may be adequate
Code 
# Extract p-values for decision display
lm_df <- data.frame(
  Test = c("LM Error", "LM Lag", "Robust LM Error", "Robust LM Lag", "SARMA"),
  Statistic = sapply(lm_tests, function(x) x$statistic),
  `p-value`  = sapply(lm_tests, function(x) x$p.value),
  check.names = FALSE
)

lm_df |>
  mutate(`p-value` = ifelse(`p-value` < 0.001, "< 0.001", round(`p-value`, 4))) |>
  kable(caption = "Lagrange Multiplier test results for spatial model selection",
        digits = 3) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Lagrange Multiplier test results for spatial model selection
Test Statistic p-value
LMerr.LMerr LM Error 5.206 0.022
LMlag.LMlag LM Lag 8.898 0.003
RLMerr.RLMerr Robust LM Error 0.044 0.834
RLMlag.RLMlag Robust LM Lag 3.736 0.053
SARMA.SARMA SARMA 8.942 0.011

5.3 Model 2: SLX — Spatially Lagged X

The SLX model adds spatially lagged versions of the predictors (but not of the outcome):

\[y = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + WX\boldsymbol{\theta} + \varepsilon\]

This is the simplest way to capture local spillovers: the crime rate in neighborhood \(i\) is affected not just by income in \(i\), but also by average income among \(i\)’s neighbors.

Code 
reg2 <- lmSLX(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)
summary(reg2)

Call:
lm(formula = formula(paste("y ~ ", paste(colnames(x)[-1], collapse = "+"))), 
    data = as.data.frame(x), weights = weights)

Residuals:
   Min     1Q Median     3Q    Max 
-36.34  -7.66  -0.01   7.98  25.57 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)   74.553      6.716   11.10  2.4e-14 ***
INC           -1.097      0.374   -2.94   0.0053 ** 
HOVAL         -0.294      0.102   -2.90   0.0059 ** 
lag.INC       -1.399      0.560   -2.50   0.0163 *  
lag.HOVAL      0.215      0.208    1.03   0.3071    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 10.9 on 44 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.611, Adjusted R-squared:  0.575 
F-statistic: 17.2 on 4 and 44 DF,  p-value: 0.0000000141

Direct and indirect effects (total impact accounting for spatial lags):

Code 
impacts_slx <- impacts(reg2, listw = queen_listw)
summary(impacts(reg2, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
Impact measures (SlX, glht, n-k):
       Direct Indirect    Total
INC   -1.0974  -1.3987 -2.49614
HOVAL -0.2944   0.2148 -0.07955
========================================================
Standard errors:
      Direct Indirect  Total
INC   0.3738   0.5601 0.4930
HOVAL 0.1017   0.2079 0.2075
========================================================
Z-values:
      Direct Indirect   Total
INC   -2.936   -2.497 -5.0634
HOVAL -2.896    1.033 -0.3834

p-values:
      Direct Indirect Total     
INC   0.0033 0.013    0.00000041
HOVAL 0.0038 0.301    0.7
Note

Interpreting impacts in the SLX model:

  • Direct effect: the effect of a unit change in \(X_i\) on \(y_i\)
  • Indirect effect: the effect of a unit change in neighbors’ \(X\) on \(y_i\) (the spillover)
  • Total effect: direct + indirect

5.4 Model 3: SAR — Spatial Autoregressive (Lag) Model

The SAR model (also called the spatial lag model) includes a spatially lagged dependent variable:

\[y = \rho W y + X\boldsymbol{\beta} + \varepsilon\]

The parameter \(\rho\) (rho) captures global spatial dependence: how much a neighborhood’s crime rate is influenced by its neighbors’ crime rates, above and beyond observed covariates. Think of it as capturing contagion, diffusion, or spillover in the outcome itself.

Code 
reg3 <- lagsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)
summary(reg3)

Call:lagsarlm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)

Residuals:
       Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
-37.652017  -5.334611   0.071473   6.107196  23.302617 

Type: lag 
Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
             Estimate Std. Error z value        Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) 45.603248   7.257404  6.2837 0.0000000003306
INC         -1.048728   0.307406 -3.4115        0.000646
HOVAL       -0.266335   0.089096 -2.9893        0.002796

Rho: 0.4233, LR test value: 9.406, p-value: 0.0021621
Asymptotic standard error: 0.1195
    z-value: 3.542, p-value: 0.00039686
Wald statistic: 12.55, p-value: 0.00039686

Log likelihood: -182.7 for lag model
ML residual variance (sigma squared): 96.86, (sigma: 9.842)
Number of observations: 49 
Number of parameters estimated: 5 
AIC: NA (not available for weighted model), (AIC for lm: 382.8)
LM test for residual autocorrelation
test value: 0.247, p-value: 0.61917
Warning

Critical point: In the SAR model, the \(\beta\) coefficients cannot be interpreted as marginal effects. Because \(y\) appears on both sides of the equation, a change in \(X_i\) triggers a chain reaction through the whole network. You must use the impacts() function to recover interpretable effects.

Code 
summary(impacts(reg3, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
Impact measures (lag, exact):
       Direct Indirect   Total
INC   -1.1009  -0.7177 -1.8186
HOVAL -0.2796  -0.1823 -0.4618
========================================================
Simulation results ( variance matrix):
Direct:

Iterations = 1:500
Thinning interval = 1 
Number of chains = 1 
Sample size per chain = 500 

1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
   plus standard error of the mean:

        Mean     SD Naive SE Time-series SE
INC   -1.103 0.3277  0.01465        0.01465
HOVAL -0.285 0.0935  0.00418        0.00418

2. Quantiles for each variable:

        2.5%    25%   50%    75%  97.5%
INC   -1.766 -1.311 -1.10 -0.886 -0.487
HOVAL -0.476 -0.349 -0.28 -0.220 -0.111

========================================================
Indirect:

Iterations = 1:500
Thinning interval = 1 
Number of chains = 1 
Sample size per chain = 500 

1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
   plus standard error of the mean:

        Mean    SD Naive SE Time-series SE
INC   -0.781 0.490  0.02192        0.02192
HOVAL -0.211 0.136  0.00606        0.00606

2. Quantiles for each variable:

        2.5%    25%    50%    75%   97.5%
INC   -1.870 -0.959 -0.668 -0.487 -0.2376
HOVAL -0.569 -0.275 -0.177 -0.113 -0.0487

========================================================
Total:

Iterations = 1:500
Thinning interval = 1 
Number of chains = 1 
Sample size per chain = 500 

1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
   plus standard error of the mean:

        Mean    SD Naive SE Time-series SE
INC   -1.884 0.674  0.03014        0.03014
HOVAL -0.497 0.203  0.00907        0.00907

2. Quantiles for each variable:

        2.5%    25%    50%    75%  97.5%
INC   -3.351 -2.202 -1.819 -1.489 -0.865
HOVAL -0.966 -0.601 -0.467 -0.349 -0.180

========================================================
Simulated standard errors
       Direct Indirect  Total
INC   0.32769   0.4903 0.6739
HOVAL 0.09351   0.1356 0.2028

Simulated z-values:
      Direct Indirect  Total
INC   -3.366   -1.594 -2.796
HOVAL -3.052   -1.559 -2.450

Simulated p-values:
      Direct  Indirect Total 
INC   0.00076 0.11     0.0052
HOVAL 0.00227 0.12     0.0143

5.5 Model 4: SEM — Spatial Error Model

The SEM models spatial dependence in the error term rather than in the outcome:

\[y = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + u, \quad u = \lambda W u + \varepsilon\]

Here \(\lambda\) (lambda) captures spatial correlation in the unmeasured factors that drive outcomes. The SEM treats spatial autocorrelation as a nuisance — something to correct for, rather than a substantive spillover to estimate. Coefficients retain their standard marginal effect interpretation.

Code 
reg4 <- errorsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)
summary(reg4)

Call:errorsarlm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-34.65998  -6.16943  -0.70623   7.75392  23.43878 

Type: error 
Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
             Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) 60.279470   5.365594 11.2344 < 2.2e-16
INC         -0.957305   0.334231 -2.8642 0.0041806
HOVAL       -0.304559   0.092047 -3.3087 0.0009372

Lambda: 0.5467, LR test value: 7.256, p-value: 0.0070679
Asymptotic standard error: 0.1381
    z-value: 3.961, p-value: 0.000074786
Wald statistic: 15.69, p-value: 0.000074786

Log likelihood: -183.7 for error model
ML residual variance (sigma squared): 97.67, (sigma: 9.883)
Number of observations: 49 
Number of parameters estimated: 5 
AIC: 377.5, (AIC for lm: 382.8)

Spatial Hausman Test — tests whether the spatial error structure is misspecified (i.e., whether OLS would have been consistent):

Code 
hausman <- Hausman.test(reg4)
hausman

    Spatial Hausman test (asymptotic)

data:  NULL
Hausman test = 5.6, df = 3, p-value = 0.1

A significant Hausman test suggests the SEM is preferred over OLS. See Pace & LeSage (2008) for technical details.

6. Extended Models: The Modern Approach

6.1 Model 5: SDM — Spatial Durbin Model

The SDM adds spatially lagged \(X\) to the SAR model:

\[y = \rho W y + X\boldsymbol{\beta} + WX\boldsymbol{\theta} + \varepsilon\]

The SDM nests both SAR and SLX as special cases. It is often preferred when theory suggests both global contagion and local covariate spillovers.

Code 
reg5 <- lagsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, type = "mixed")
summary(reg5)

Call:lagsarlm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, 
    type = "mixed")

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-37.31199  -6.49556  -0.22971   6.17872  22.74795 

Type: mixed 
Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
             Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) 44.320003  13.045474  3.3973 0.0006804
INC         -0.919906   0.334742 -2.7481 0.0059941
HOVAL       -0.297129   0.090416 -3.2863 0.0010153
lag.INC     -0.583913   0.574224 -1.0169 0.3092140
lag.HOVAL    0.257684   0.187235  1.3763 0.1687404

Rho: 0.4035, LR test value: 4.663, p-value: 0.030825
Asymptotic standard error: 0.1613
    z-value: 2.501, p-value: 0.012392
Wald statistic: 6.254, p-value: 0.012392

Log likelihood: -181.6 for mixed model
ML residual variance (sigma squared): 93.27, (sigma: 9.658)
Number of observations: 49 
Number of parameters estimated: 7 
AIC: NA (not available for weighted model), (AIC for lm: 379.9)
LM test for residual autocorrelation
test value: 0.3626, p-value: 0.54708
Code 
impacts_sdm <- impacts(reg5, listw = queen_listw)
summary(impacts(reg5, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
Impact measures (mixed, exact):
      Direct Indirect    Total
INC   -1.025  -1.4959 -2.52091
HOVAL -0.282   0.2158 -0.06612
========================================================
Simulation results ( variance matrix):
Direct:

Iterations = 1:500
Thinning interval = 1 
Number of chains = 1 
Sample size per chain = 500 

1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
   plus standard error of the mean:

        Mean     SD Naive SE Time-series SE
INC   -1.024 0.3335  0.01491        0.01491
HOVAL -0.284 0.0924  0.00413        0.00413

2. Quantiles for each variable:

        2.5%    25%    50%    75%  97.5%
INC   -1.657 -1.267 -1.012 -0.793 -0.393
HOVAL -0.473 -0.343 -0.282 -0.221 -0.112

========================================================
Indirect:

Iterations = 1:500
Thinning interval = 1 
Number of chains = 1 
Sample size per chain = 500 

1. Empirical mean and standard deviation for each variable,
   plus standard error of the mean:

        Mean    SD Naive SE Time-series SE
INC   -1.490 0.750   0.0335         0.0335
HOVAL  0.208 0.297   0.0133         0.0133

2. Quantiles for each variable:

       2.5%     25%    50%    75%   97.5%
INC   -2.90 -1.9891 -1.489 -0.978 -0.0342
HOVAL -0.34  0.0169  0.179  0.393  0.8645

Iterations = 1:500
Thinning interval = 1 
Number of chains = 1

   plus standard error of the mean:

         Mean    SD Naive SE Time-series SE
INC   -2.5146 0.774   0.0346         0.0346
HOVAL -0.0762 0.324   0.0145         0.0145

2. Quantiles for each variable:

        2.5%    25%     50%    75%  97.5%
INC   -4.152 -2.973 -2.4951 -1.981 -1.027
HOVAL -0.679 -0.287 -0.0783  0.115  0.641

Simulated standard errors
       Direct Indirect  Total
INC   0.33351   0.7497 0.7744
HOVAL 0.09244   0.2973 0.3236

Simulated z-values:
      Direct Indirect   Total
INC   -3.072  -1.9878 -3.2472
HOVAL -3.072   0.6986 -0.2356

Simulated p-values:
      Direct Indirect Total 
INC   0.0021 0.047    0.0012
HOVAL 0.0021 0.485    0.8138

6.2 Model 6: SDEM — Spatial Durbin Error Model

The SDEM adds spatially lagged \(X\) to the SEM:

\[y = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + WX\boldsymbol{\theta} + u, \quad u = \lambda W u + \varepsilon\]

The SDEM is useful when you believe spillovers are local (captured by \(WX\)) but the error structure is globally autocorrelated.

Code 
reg6 <- errorsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, etype = "emixed")
summary(reg6)

Call:errorsarlm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, 
    etype = "emixed")

Residuals:
       Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
-37.180636  -6.588766  -0.091176   6.387284  23.180560 

Type: error 
Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
             Estimate Std. Error z value  Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) 73.645083   8.723875  8.4418 < 2.2e-16
INC         -1.052258   0.321279 -3.2752  0.001056
HOVAL       -0.278174   0.091142 -3.0521  0.002272
lag.INC     -1.204876   0.573642 -2.1004  0.035694
lag.HOVAL    0.131245   0.207245  0.6333  0.526548

Lambda: 0.4036, LR test value: 4.383, p-value: 0.036295
Asymptotic standard error: 0.1635
    z-value: 2.468, p-value: 0.013584
Wald statistic: 6.091, p-value: 0.013584

Log likelihood: -181.8 for error model
ML residual variance (sigma squared): 93.8, (sigma: 9.685)
Number of observations: 49 
Number of parameters estimated: 7 
AIC: 377.6, (AIC for lm: 379.9)
Code 
summary(impacts(reg6, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
Impact measures (SDEM, glht, n):
       Direct Indirect   Total
INC   -1.0523  -1.2049 -2.2571
HOVAL -0.2782   0.1312 -0.1469
========================================================
Standard errors:
       Direct Indirect  Total
INC   0.32128   0.5736 0.6326
HOVAL 0.09114   0.2072 0.2373
========================================================
Z-values:
      Direct Indirect   Total
INC   -3.275  -2.1004 -3.5680
HOVAL -3.052   0.6333 -0.6192

p-values:
      Direct Indirect Total  
INC   0.0011 0.036    0.00036
HOVAL 0.0023 0.527    0.53578

6.3 Model 7: SARAR (SAC / Kelejian-Prucha)

The SARAR model includes both a spatial lag in \(y\) and a spatial lag in the error:

\[y = \rho W y + X\boldsymbol{\beta} + u, \quad u = \lambda W u + \varepsilon\]

This is the most general model in this family and is also called the SAC or Cliff-Ord model.

Code 
reg7 <- sacsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, type = "sac")
summary(reg7)

Call:sacsarlm(formula = reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, 
    type = "sac")

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-37.35601  -5.04457  -0.18999   6.71177  23.28400 

Type: sac 
Coefficients: (asymptotic standard errors) 
             Estimate Std. Error z value    Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) 47.915359   9.985548  4.7985 0.000001599
INC         -1.042749   0.328628 -3.1730    0.001509
HOVAL       -0.279841   0.090699 -3.0854    0.002033

Rho: 0.3694
Asymptotic standard error: 0.1963
    z-value: 1.882, p-value: 0.059817
Lambda: 0.1464
Asymptotic standard error: 0.301
    z-value: 0.4864, p-value: 0.62668

LR test value: 9.644, p-value: 0.0080489

Log likelihood: -182.6 for sac model
ML residual variance (sigma squared): 97.04, (sigma: 9.851)
Number of observations: 49 
Number of parameters estimated: 6 
AIC: NA (not available for weighted model), (AIC for lm: 382.8)
Code 
impacts(reg7, listw = queen_listw)
Impact measures (sac, exact):
      Direct Indirect   Total
INC    -1.08  -0.5731 -1.6535
HOVAL  -0.29  -0.1538 -0.4438

7. Model Comparison

7.1 Coefficient Summary Table

Code 
# Extract key statistics from each model
model_stats <- data.frame(
  Model = c("OLS", "SLX", "SAR", "SEM", "SDM", "SDEM", "SARAR"),
  AIC   = c(AIC(reg1), AIC(reg2), AIC(reg3), AIC(reg4), AIC(reg5), AIC(reg6), AIC(reg7)),
  LogLik = c(logLik(reg1), logLik(reg2), logLik(reg3), logLik(reg4),
             logLik(reg5), logLik(reg6), logLik(reg7))
)

model_stats |>
  arrange(AIC) |>
  mutate(AIC    = round(AIC, 2),
         LogLik = round(LogLik, 2)) |>
  kable(caption = "Model comparison: AIC and log-likelihood",
        col.names = c("Model", "AIC", "Log-Likelihood")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#e8f5e9")
Model comparison: AIC and log-likelihood
Model AIC Log-Likelihood
SAR 375.4 -182.7
SARAR 377.1 -182.6
SDM 377.3 -181.6
SEM 377.5 -183.8
SDEM 377.6 -181.8
SLX 379.9 -184.0
OLS 382.8 -187.4
Note

AIC interpretation: Lower AIC = better fit. The highlighted row is the best-fitting model. Note that AIC comparisons are only valid across models estimated on the same data; the spatial models are estimated by maximum likelihood while OLS uses least squares.

7.2 Residual Maps

Mapping residuals is an essential diagnostic — even after fitting spatial models, we should check whether unexplained spatial structure remains.

Code 
columbus$resid_ols <- residuals(reg1)
columbus$resid_sar <- residuals(reg3)

p_ols <- tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("resid_ols",
          style = "jenks", n = 5,
          palette = "RdBu", midpoint = 0,
          title = "OLS residuals") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.4) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "OLS", main.title.size = 0.9, frame = FALSE,
            legend.outside = FALSE)

p_sar <- tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("resid_sar",
          style = "jenks", n = 5,
          palette = "RdBu", midpoint = 0,
          title = "SAR residuals") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.4) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "SAR", main.title.size = 0.9, frame = FALSE,
            legend.outside = FALSE)

tmap_arrange(p_ols, p_sar, ncol = 2)

Residual Moran’s I comparison:

Code 
moran_ols_resid <- moran.test(residuals(reg1), listw = queen_listw)
moran_sar_resid <- moran.test(residuals(reg3), listw = queen_listw)

cat("OLS residuals — Moran's I:", round(moran_ols_resid$estimate[1], 4),
    "  p =", round(moran_ols_resid$p.value, 4), "\n")
OLS residuals — Moran's I: 0.2221   p = 0.004
Code 
cat("SAR residuals — Moran's I:", round(moran_sar_resid$estimate[1], 4),
    "  p =", round(moran_sar_resid$p.value, 4), "\n")
SAR residuals — Moran's I: 0.0331   p = 0.2733

8. Understanding Spillover Effects

One of the most important — and often overlooked — aspects of spatial regression is the distinction between direct and indirect effects.

8.1 The Feedback Problem

In the SAR model, a change in income in neighborhood A affects:

  1. Crime in A directly (direct effect)
  2. Crime in A’s neighbors (first-order spillover)
  3. Crime in A’s neighbors’ neighbors — because they are affected by A’s neighbors (second-order spillover)
  4. Eventually, crime in A itself — because A is affected by its neighbors, who are now affected (feedback effect)

This is why simple \(\beta\) coefficients in the SAR model are uninterpretable. The impacts() function from spatialreg computes the full decomposition.

8.2 Visualizing the Impact Decomposition

Code 
impacts_sar <- impacts(reg3, listw = queen_listw, R = 500)

varnames <- attr(impacts_sar, "bnames")
direct   <- as.numeric(impacts_sar$res$direct)
indirect <- as.numeric(impacts_sar$res$indirect)
total    <- as.numeric(impacts_sar$res$total)

imp_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = factor(rep(varnames, 3)),
  Effect   = rep(c("Direct", "Indirect", "Total"), each = length(varnames)),
  Estimate = c(direct, indirect, total)
)

ggplot(imp_df, aes(x = Variable, y = Estimate, fill = Effect)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Direct" = "#1976D2", "Indirect" = "#E64A19", "Total" = "#388E3C")) +
  labs(title = "SAR Model: Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects",
       x = NULL, y = "Effect on Crime Rate",
       fill = "Effect type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

9. Conceptual Map of Spatial Models

The seven models we have covered can be organized along two dimensions:

No spatial lag in \(y\) Spatial lag in \(y\) (\(\rho Wy\))
No lag in \(X\) or \(u\) OLS SAR
Lag in \(X\) only SLX SDM
Lag in \(u\) only SEM SARAR
Lag in \(X\) and \(u\) SDEM Manski (all-inclusive)

Choosing among them is primarily a theoretical question — what do you believe is the data-generating process? — with LM tests as a useful empirical guide when theory is ambiguous.

10. Exercises

Exercise 10.1 — Reproducing the LM Decision Rule

Run the full sequence: OLS → lm.morantest() → lm.LMtests() for the Columbus dataset. Based on the robust LM test results, which model would you select? Does your choice match the AIC ranking you produced in Section 7.1?

Exercise 10.2 — Comparing Contiguity Structures

Re-fit the SAR model using rook contiguity (rook_listw) instead of queen. Compare:

  • The estimated \(\rho\)
  • The direct and indirect effects
  • The model AIC

What does this sensitivity tell you about the importance of the weights matrix choice?

Exercise 10.3 — Spillover Interpretation

Using the SDM impacts output, interpret the indirect effect of INC (household income). Write 2–3 sentences suitable for inclusion in a policy brief: what does the spillover mean in plain language, and what policy implication might follow?

11. Discussion Questions

  1. Theory before models: A colleague says, “I’ll just run all eight models and pick the one with the best AIC.” What is the problem with this approach, and what should guide model selection instead?

  2. Spillovers and policy: You find a significant indirect effect of neighborhood poverty on crime, meaning high poverty in one area increases crime in neighboring areas. What does this imply for place-based policy interventions (e.g., targeted poverty reduction in one neighborhood)?

  3. The weights matrix as a model assumption: We treated queen contiguity as given, but spatial weights encode a theoretical claim about the nature of spatial interaction. What alternative weight structures might make sense for studying (a) infectious disease transmission, (b) alcohol availability and harm, (c) emergency response times?

  4. Spatial error vs. spatial lag: In a study of opioid overdose deaths at the county level, would you expect spatial dependence to manifest more in the outcome (SAR) or in the error (SEM)? What mechanisms would drive each?

References

  • Anselin, L. (1988). Spatial Econometrics: Methods and Models. Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic.
  • Anselin, L. (2003). Spatial externalities, spatial multipliers, and spatial econometrics. International Regional Science Review, 26(2), 153–166.
  • Burkey, M.L. (2010). Geographic Access and Demand in the Market for Alcohol. The Review of Regional Studies, 40(2), 159–179.
  • LeSage, J.P. & Pace, R.K. (2009). Introduction to Spatial Econometrics. Boca Raton: CRC Press.
  • Pace, R.K. & LeSage, J.P. (2008). A spatial Hausman test. Economics Letters, 101(3), 282–284.
  • Ward, M.D. & Gleditsch, K.S. (2008). Spatial Regression Models. Thousand Oaks: SAGE.
Source Code
---
title: "Spatial Regression"
subtitle: "Spatial Epidemiology — Lehigh University"
author: "Eric Delmelle"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 6
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: false
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)

# Presentation options
options(digits = 4, scipen = 7)
```

---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

1. Explain **why OLS fails** when spatial autocorrelation is present in the residuals
2. Distinguish between a **spatial lag model (SAR)**, a **spatial error model (SEM)**, and their extensions
3. Use **Lagrange Multiplier tests** to choose between model specifications
4. Fit and interpret four core spatial regression models in R
5. Compute and explain **direct and indirect (spillover) effects** in spatial models
6. Appreciate the implications of **spatial spillovers** for public health policy

---

## 1. Why Spatial Regression?

In earlier sessions we tested for spatial autocorrelation using Moran's I and Local Indicators of Spatial Association (LISA). We found that health outcomes — disease rates, accessibility scores, socioeconomic exposures — are rarely spatially random. Nearby places tend to be more alike than distant places.

This is not just a descriptive curiosity. When we model health outcomes using **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** regression, we assume that the residuals are independent across observations. That assumption almost never holds for spatial health data. Ignoring it has real consequences:

- Standard errors are **biased**, leading to incorrect inference
- Coefficients can be **inconsistent** (SAR case)
- We may miss important **spillover effects** — mechanisms by which conditions in one place influence outcomes in neighboring places

Spatial regression models explicitly account for these dependencies. Rather than being a nuisance correction, spatial structure often carries substantive meaning.

### 1.1 A Running Example: Liquor Sales and Social Harm

We use the **NCVACO dataset** — county-level data from North Carolina and Virginia — originally assembled by economist Mark Burkey to study the geography of alcohol markets. This dataset is analytically rich because it contains:

- A plausible **outcome variable**: DUI arrests per 1,000 adults (`DUI1802`)
- **Access measures**: distance to nearest liquor outlet (`BKGRTOABC`), distance to nearest bar (`BKGRTOMIX`)
- **Socioeconomic covariates**: per capita income, unemployment, college enrollment, poverty
- **Cultural variables**: % Southern Baptist (`BAPTISTSP`), % armed forces (`ARMEDP`)

The spatial dimension is central: liquor market regulation varies sharply across state borders; commuting patterns connect counties; enforcement intensity may spill across county lines.

::: {.callout-note}
**Data source:** Burkey, M.L. (2010). *Geographic Access and Demand in the Market for Alcohol.* The Review of Regional Studies, 40(2), 159–179. Data available via [BurkeyAcademy's GIS & Spatial Econometrics Project](https://spatial.burkeyacademy.com/).
:::

---

## 2. Data Setup


```{r load-packages}
library(sf)
library(spdep)
library(spatialreg)
library(tidyverse)
library(tmap)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

```{r load-data}
# Load the NCVACO dataset bundled with spatialreg
data("boston", package = "spatialreg")  # placeholder check
data("nc.sids", package = "spData", envir = environment())

# The NCVACO data is distributed with the spdep package as a shapefile
# We load it directly from the package
nc_path <- system.file("shapes/columbus.shp", package = "spData")

# Use Columbus Ohio crime data (bundled, similar structure) as a 
# self-contained teaching substitute — same model family, clean reproducibility
columbus <- st_read(system.file("shapes/columbus.shp", package = "spData"),
                    quiet = TRUE)
```

::: {.callout-tip}
**Self-contained reproducibility:** This document uses the `columbus` dataset bundled with the `spData` package — no external file downloads required. The Columbus dataset contains neighborhood-level crime, income, and housing data for Columbus, Ohio, and is a classic benchmark in spatial econometrics. The same model logic applies directly to the NCVACO alcohol dataset or any county-level health outcome data.
:::

### 2.1 Variable Overview

The Columbus dataset contains 49 neighborhoods with the following key variables:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `CRIME`  | Residential burglaries and vehicle thefts per 1,000 households |
| `INC`    | Household income (in $1,000s) |
| `HOVAL`  | Housing value (in $1,000s) |
| `OPEN`   | Open space in neighborhood |
| `PLUMB`  | % housing units without plumbing |
| `DISCBD` | Distance to Central Business District |

```{r data-summary}
columbus |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(CRIME, INC, HOVAL, OPEN, PLUMB, DISCBD) |>
  summary() |>
  kable(caption = "Summary statistics: Columbus neighborhood dataset") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### 2.2 Spatial Distribution of Crime

```{r map-crime}
#| fig-cap: "Residential crime rate by neighborhood, Columbus, Ohio."

tmap_mode("plot")

tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("CRIME",
          style = "quantile",
          n = 5,
          palette = "YlOrRd",
          title = "Crime per 1,000 HH") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(
    main.title = "Residential Crime, Columbus OH",
    main.title.size = 1,
    legend.outside = FALSE,
    frame = FALSE
  )
```

---

## 3. Constructing Spatial Weights

Before fitting any spatial model, we need to define **who is a neighbor** of whom, and how to weight those relationships.

### 3.1 Queen Contiguity

Two polygons are **queen contiguous** if they share any boundary point (including corners). This is the most common choice for areal data.

```{r weights}
# Queen contiguity neighbor list
queen_nb <- poly2nb(columbus, queen = TRUE)

# Rook contiguity (shared edges only, no corners)
rook_nb <- poly2nb(columbus, queen = FALSE)

# Convert to row-standardized listw object
queen_listw <- nb2listw(queen_nb, style = "W")
rook_listw  <- nb2listw(rook_nb,  style = "W")

# Summary
summary(queen_nb)
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Row standardization (`style = "W"`)** means each row of the weights matrix sums to 1. This makes the spatial lag $\mathbf{Wy}$ interpretable as a **local average** of neighbors' values — convenient for interpretation but it does change the scale of $\rho$.
:::

### 3.2 Symmetry Check

```{r symmetry}
is.symmetric.nb(queen_nb)
is.symmetric.nb(rook_nb)
```

Both contiguity structures are symmetric: if A is a neighbor of B, then B is a neighbor of A. This is a prerequisite for many spatial regression estimators.

### 3.3 Visualizing the Neighbor Network

```{r plot-neighbors}
#| fig-cap: "Queen contiguity neighbor links overlaid on Columbus neighborhoods."
#| fig-height: 5

# Get centroids for plotting
coords <- st_centroid(st_geometry(columbus))

plot(st_geometry(columbus), border = "grey70", col = "grey95",
     main = "Queen Contiguity Neighbor Network")
plot(queen_nb, coords, add = TRUE, col = "#2196F3", lwd = 0.8)
```

---

## 4. Testing for Spatial Autocorrelation

Before choosing a spatial model, we should confirm that spatial autocorrelation is actually present in our outcome and, after OLS, in our residuals.

### 4.1 Moran's I on the Outcome

```{r morans-i-outcome}
moran_crime <- moran.test(columbus$CRIME, listw = queen_listw)
moran_crime
```

```{r morans-i-display}
cat(sprintf(
  "Moran's I = %.4f  |  p-value = %.4e\n",
  moran_crime$estimate["Moran I statistic"],
  moran_crime$p.value
))
```

A positive, significant Moran's I confirms spatial clustering in the crime rate — high-crime neighborhoods tend to be surrounded by other high-crime neighborhoods.

---

## 5. The Four Core Spatial Regression Models

All spatial regression models in the classical framework begin from a standard linear specification and then add spatial structure. We define our regression equation once:

$$\text{CRIME}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{INC}_i + \beta_2 \cdot \text{HOVAL}_i + \varepsilon_i$$

```{r reg-equation}
reg_eq <- CRIME ~ INC + HOVAL
```

### 5.1 Model 1: OLS (Baseline)

OLS is our benchmark. We estimate it first, then test whether its residuals are spatially autocorrelated.

```{r ols}
reg1 <- lm(reg_eq, data = columbus)
summary(reg1)
```

**Residual spatial autocorrelation test:**

```{r ols-moran}
lm_moran <- lm.morantest(reg1, listw = queen_listw)
lm_moran
```

Significant residual autocorrelation confirms that OLS is misspecified — something spatial is going on that our covariates do not capture.

```{r ols-resid-map}
#| fig-cap: "OLS residuals mapped across Columbus neighborhoods. Spatial clustering of positive and negative residuals is visible, confirming the Moran's I result."

columbus$resid_ols <- residuals(reg1)

tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("resid_ols",
          style = "jenks", n = 5,
          palette = "RdBu", midpoint = 0,
          title = "OLS residuals") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.5) +
  tm_layout(
    main.title = "OLS Residuals",
    main.title.size = 1,
    frame = FALSE,
    legend.outside = FALSE
  )
```

### 5.2 Model Selection via LM Tests

The **Lagrange Multiplier (LM) tests** are the standard decision tool for choosing between a spatial lag and a spatial error specification.

```{r lm-tests}
lm_tests <- lm.LMtests(reg1, queen_listw,
                        test = c("LMerr", "LMlag", "RLMerr", "RLMlag", "SARMA"))
summary(lm_tests)
```

::: {.callout-important}
**Decision rule (Anselin 1988):**

1. If only **LMlag** is significant → use SAR (spatial lag model)
2. If only **LMerr** is significant → use SEM (spatial error model)  
3. If both are significant → compare the **robust versions** (RLMlag vs. RLMerr); choose the one with the smaller p-value
4. If neither is significant → OLS may be adequate
:::

```{r lm-decision}
# Extract p-values for decision display
lm_df <- data.frame(
  Test = c("LM Error", "LM Lag", "Robust LM Error", "Robust LM Lag", "SARMA"),
  Statistic = sapply(lm_tests, function(x) x$statistic),
  `p-value`  = sapply(lm_tests, function(x) x$p.value),
  check.names = FALSE
)

lm_df |>
  mutate(`p-value` = ifelse(`p-value` < 0.001, "< 0.001", round(`p-value`, 4))) |>
  kable(caption = "Lagrange Multiplier test results for spatial model selection",
        digits = 3) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
```

### 5.3 Model 2: SLX — Spatially Lagged X

The **SLX model** adds spatially lagged versions of the predictors (but not of the outcome):

$$y = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + WX\boldsymbol{\theta} + \varepsilon$$

This is the simplest way to capture **local spillovers**: the crime rate in neighborhood $i$ is affected not just by income in $i$, but also by average income among $i$'s neighbors.

```{r slx}
reg2 <- lmSLX(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)
summary(reg2)
```

**Direct and indirect effects** (total impact accounting for spatial lags):

```{r slx-impacts}
impacts_slx <- impacts(reg2, listw = queen_listw)
summary(impacts(reg2, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
```

::: {.callout-note}
**Interpreting impacts in the SLX model:**

- **Direct effect**: the effect of a unit change in $X_i$ on $y_i$
- **Indirect effect**: the effect of a unit change in neighbors' $X$ on $y_i$ (the spillover)
- **Total effect**: direct + indirect
:::

### 5.4 Model 3: SAR — Spatial Autoregressive (Lag) Model

The **SAR model** (also called the spatial lag model) includes a spatially lagged dependent variable:

$$y = \rho W y + X\boldsymbol{\beta} + \varepsilon$$

The parameter $\rho$ (rho) captures **global spatial dependence**: how much a neighborhood's crime rate is influenced by its neighbors' crime rates, above and beyond observed covariates. Think of it as capturing contagion, diffusion, or spillover in the outcome itself.

```{r sar}
reg3 <- lagsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)
summary(reg3)
```

::: {.callout-warning}
**Critical point:** In the SAR model, the $\beta$ coefficients **cannot be interpreted as marginal effects**. Because $y$ appears on both sides of the equation, a change in $X_i$ triggers a chain reaction through the whole network. You must use the `impacts()` function to recover interpretable effects.
:::

```{r sar-impacts}
summary(impacts(reg3, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
```

### 5.5 Model 4: SEM — Spatial Error Model

The **SEM** models spatial dependence in the **error term** rather than in the outcome:

$$y = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + u, \quad u = \lambda W u + \varepsilon$$

Here $\lambda$ (lambda) captures spatial correlation in the unmeasured factors that drive outcomes. The SEM treats spatial autocorrelation as a **nuisance** — something to correct for, rather than a substantive spillover to estimate. Coefficients retain their standard marginal effect interpretation.

```{r sem}
reg4 <- errorsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw)
summary(reg4)
```

**Spatial Hausman Test** — tests whether the spatial error structure is misspecified (i.e., whether OLS would have been consistent):

```{r hausman}
hausman <- Hausman.test(reg4)
hausman
```

A significant Hausman test suggests the SEM is preferred over OLS. See [Pace & LeSage (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S016517650800270X) for technical details.

---

## 6. Extended Models: The Modern Approach

### 6.1 Model 5: SDM — Spatial Durbin Model

The **SDM** adds spatially lagged $X$ to the SAR model:

$$y = \rho W y + X\boldsymbol{\beta} + WX\boldsymbol{\theta} + \varepsilon$$

The SDM nests both SAR and SLX as special cases. It is often preferred when theory suggests both global contagion *and* local covariate spillovers.

```{r sdm}
reg5 <- lagsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, type = "mixed")
summary(reg5)
```

```{r sdm-impacts}
impacts_sdm <- impacts(reg5, listw = queen_listw)
summary(impacts(reg5, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
```

### 6.2 Model 6: SDEM — Spatial Durbin Error Model

The **SDEM** adds spatially lagged $X$ to the SEM:

$$y = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + WX\boldsymbol{\theta} + u, \quad u = \lambda W u + \varepsilon$$

The SDEM is useful when you believe spillovers are **local** (captured by $WX$) but the error structure is globally autocorrelated.

```{r sdem}
reg6 <- errorsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, etype = "emixed")
summary(reg6)
```

```{r sdem-impacts}
summary(impacts(reg6, listw = queen_listw, R = 500), zstats = TRUE)
```

### 6.3 Model 7: SARAR (SAC / Kelejian-Prucha)

The **SARAR** model includes both a spatial lag in $y$ and a spatial lag in the error:

$$y = \rho W y + X\boldsymbol{\beta} + u, \quad u = \lambda W u + \varepsilon$$

This is the most general model in this family and is also called the **SAC** or **Cliff-Ord** model.

```{r sarar}
reg7 <- sacsarlm(reg_eq, data = columbus, listw = queen_listw, type = "sac")
summary(reg7)
```

```{r sarar-impacts}
impacts(reg7, listw = queen_listw)
```

---

## 7. Model Comparison

### 7.1 Coefficient Summary Table

```{r model-table}
# Extract key statistics from each model
model_stats <- data.frame(
  Model = c("OLS", "SLX", "SAR", "SEM", "SDM", "SDEM", "SARAR"),
  AIC   = c(AIC(reg1), AIC(reg2), AIC(reg3), AIC(reg4), AIC(reg5), AIC(reg6), AIC(reg7)),
  LogLik = c(logLik(reg1), logLik(reg2), logLik(reg3), logLik(reg4),
             logLik(reg5), logLik(reg6), logLik(reg7))
)

model_stats |>
  arrange(AIC) |>
  mutate(AIC    = round(AIC, 2),
         LogLik = round(LogLik, 2)) |>
  kable(caption = "Model comparison: AIC and log-likelihood",
        col.names = c("Model", "AIC", "Log-Likelihood")) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#e8f5e9")
```

::: {.callout-note}
**AIC interpretation:** Lower AIC = better fit. The highlighted row is the best-fitting model. Note that AIC comparisons are only valid across models estimated on the same data; the spatial models are estimated by maximum likelihood while OLS uses least squares.
:::

### 7.2 Residual Maps

Mapping residuals is an essential diagnostic — even after fitting spatial models, we should check whether unexplained spatial structure remains.

```{r residual-maps}
#| fig-cap: "OLS vs. SAR residuals — note the reduction in spatial clustering in the SAR model."
#| fig-height: 5

columbus$resid_ols <- residuals(reg1)
columbus$resid_sar <- residuals(reg3)

p_ols <- tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("resid_ols",
          style = "jenks", n = 5,
          palette = "RdBu", midpoint = 0,
          title = "OLS residuals") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.4) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "OLS", main.title.size = 0.9, frame = FALSE,
            legend.outside = FALSE)

p_sar <- tm_shape(columbus) +
  tm_fill("resid_sar",
          style = "jenks", n = 5,
          palette = "RdBu", midpoint = 0,
          title = "SAR residuals") +
  tm_borders(col = "white", lwd = 0.4) +
  tm_layout(main.title = "SAR", main.title.size = 0.9, frame = FALSE,
            legend.outside = FALSE)

tmap_arrange(p_ols, p_sar, ncol = 2)
```

**Residual Moran's I comparison:**

```{r resid-moran}
moran_ols_resid <- moran.test(residuals(reg1), listw = queen_listw)
moran_sar_resid <- moran.test(residuals(reg3), listw = queen_listw)

cat("OLS residuals — Moran's I:", round(moran_ols_resid$estimate[1], 4),
    "  p =", round(moran_ols_resid$p.value, 4), "\n")
cat("SAR residuals — Moran's I:", round(moran_sar_resid$estimate[1], 4),
    "  p =", round(moran_sar_resid$p.value, 4), "\n")
```

---

## 8. Understanding Spillover Effects

One of the most important — and often overlooked — aspects of spatial regression is the distinction between **direct** and **indirect effects**.

### 8.1 The Feedback Problem

In the SAR model, a change in income in neighborhood A affects:

1. Crime in A directly (direct effect)
2. Crime in A's neighbors (first-order spillover)
3. Crime in A's neighbors' neighbors — because they are affected by A's neighbors (second-order spillover)
4. Eventually, crime in A itself — because A is affected by its neighbors, who are now affected (feedback effect)

This is why simple $\beta$ coefficients in the SAR model are uninterpretable. The `impacts()` function from `spatialreg` computes the full decomposition.

### 8.2 Visualizing the Impact Decomposition

```{r impact-viz}
#| fig-cap: "Direct vs. indirect effects for the SAR model. Indirect (spillover) effects can be substantial."

impacts_sar <- impacts(reg3, listw = queen_listw, R = 500)

varnames <- attr(impacts_sar, "bnames")
direct   <- as.numeric(impacts_sar$res$direct)
indirect <- as.numeric(impacts_sar$res$indirect)
total    <- as.numeric(impacts_sar$res$total)

imp_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = factor(rep(varnames, 3)),
  Effect   = rep(c("Direct", "Indirect", "Total"), each = length(varnames)),
  Estimate = c(direct, indirect, total)
)

ggplot(imp_df, aes(x = Variable, y = Estimate, fill = Effect)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Direct" = "#1976D2", "Indirect" = "#E64A19", "Total" = "#388E3C")) +
  labs(title = "SAR Model: Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects",
       x = NULL, y = "Effect on Crime Rate",
       fill = "Effect type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")
```

---

## 9. Conceptual Map of Spatial Models

The seven models we have covered can be organized along two dimensions:

| | **No spatial lag in $y$** | **Spatial lag in $y$ ($\rho Wy$)** |
|---|---|---|
| **No lag in $X$ or $u$** | OLS | SAR |
| **Lag in $X$ only** | SLX | SDM |
| **Lag in $u$ only** | SEM | SARAR |
| **Lag in $X$ and $u$** | SDEM | Manski (all-inclusive) |

Choosing among them is primarily a **theoretical** question — what do you believe is the data-generating process? — with LM tests as a useful empirical guide when theory is ambiguous.

---

## 10. Exercises

::: {.callout-tip}
### Exercise 10.1 — Reproducing the LM Decision Rule

Run the full sequence: OLS → `lm.morantest()` → `lm.LMtests()` for the Columbus dataset. Based on the robust LM test results, which model would you select? Does your choice match the AIC ranking you produced in Section 7.1?
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
### Exercise 10.2 — Comparing Contiguity Structures

Re-fit the SAR model using **rook** contiguity (`rook_listw`) instead of queen. Compare:

- The estimated $\rho$
- The direct and indirect effects
- The model AIC

What does this sensitivity tell you about the importance of the weights matrix choice?
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
### Exercise 10.3 — Spillover Interpretation

Using the SDM impacts output, interpret the **indirect effect** of `INC` (household income). Write 2–3 sentences suitable for inclusion in a policy brief: what does the spillover mean in plain language, and what policy implication might follow?
:::



---

## 11. Discussion Questions

1. **Theory before models**: A colleague says, "I'll just run all eight models and pick the one with the best AIC." What is the problem with this approach, and what should guide model selection instead?

2. **Spillovers and policy**: You find a significant *indirect* effect of neighborhood poverty on crime, meaning high poverty in one area increases crime in *neighboring* areas. What does this imply for place-based policy interventions (e.g., targeted poverty reduction in one neighborhood)?

3. **The weights matrix as a model assumption**: We treated queen contiguity as given, but spatial weights encode a theoretical claim about the nature of spatial interaction. What alternative weight structures might make sense for studying (a) infectious disease transmission, (b) alcohol availability and harm, (c) emergency response times?

4. **Spatial error vs. spatial lag**: In a study of opioid overdose deaths at the county level, would you expect spatial dependence to manifest more in the *outcome* (SAR) or in the *error* (SEM)? What mechanisms would drive each?

---

## References

- Anselin, L. (1988). *Spatial Econometrics: Methods and Models.* Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic.
- Anselin, L. (2003). Spatial externalities, spatial multipliers, and spatial econometrics. *International Regional Science Review*, 26(2), 153–166.
- Burkey, M.L. (2010). Geographic Access and Demand in the Market for Alcohol. *The Review of Regional Studies*, 40(2), 159–179.
- LeSage, J.P. & Pace, R.K. (2009). *Introduction to Spatial Econometrics.* Boca Raton: CRC Press.
- Pace, R.K. & LeSage, J.P. (2008). A spatial Hausman test. *Economics Letters*, 101(3), 282–284.
- Ward, M.D. & Gleditsch, K.S. (2008). *Spatial Regression Models.* Thousand Oaks: SAGE.
